title: Drone warfare with a moral code
gist: Sir David Omand, former GCHQ director, argues that recent advances in generative and agentic AI now make it plausible to train autonomous weapons systems to reason within international humanitarian law's necessity/proportionality framework — reversing his 2014 conclusion that lethal autonomy was ethically infeasible — because hypersonic missiles and drone swarms are forcing human oversight from 'in the loop' to 'on the loop,' and Russia and China are already fielding fully autonomous lethal systems with no realistic prospect of a ban treaty.

themes.theme: From 'in the loop' to 'on the loop'

insights.claim: Modern warfare's speed — hypersonic missiles that can separate radar warning from impact by mere seconds, jamming, and coordinated drone swarms — is forcing humans out of moment-to-moment lethal decisions not by policy choice but by the physical constraints of the operational environment.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Explains the structural driver behind the AI-autonomy push in defence procurement — demand for autonomous C2/targeting systems should grow regardless of ethical debate, because the threat environment leaves no alternative.
insights.quote: there simply isn’t enough time for a human to assess the situation and react in time
insights.locator: When an enemy can deploy weapons such as hypersonic missiles

insights.claim: Omand frames the shift as moving human oversight from 'in the loop' (directly deciding each engagement) to 'on the loop' (setting rules in advance and supervising a system that acts autonomously, intervening only when something goes wrong).
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Gives a crisp vocabulary — 'in the loop' vs 'on the loop' — for classifying autonomy levels in defence-tech investment theses and regulatory debate.
insights.quote: Increasingly, the role is becoming on the loop
insights.locator: Traditionally, a human would be in the loop

insights.claim: In 2014, Omand chaired a UK Policy Commission on drones (with retired RAF officers, ethicists, academics and engineers) that concluded it was not possible to programme lethal autonomous weapons for ground operations in a way consistent with the Geneva Conventions' distinction and proportionality requirements.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Establishes the expert-consensus baseline Omand is now reversing — a useful marker for how fast the expert view on AI-in-warfare ethics is shifting, a leading indicator for defence-AI regulation.
insights.quote: It would not be possible to programme lethal autonomous weapons systems for ground operations consistent with the Geneva conventions.
insights.locator: In 2014, I chaired a Policy Commission

insights.claim: Omand has reversed that 2014 judgment: he now believes it should be possible to train an AI to act ethically, crediting the recent leap in generative AI, agentic AI, and complex-coding capability as the enabling change.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: A named former GCHQ director publicly endorsing autonomous lethal AI as ethically tractable is a signal event for defence-AI policy and procurement, worth tracking as a legitimising data point for the sector.
insights.quote: I have had to change my mind about autonomous weapons systems. I think it should be possible to train an AI to act ethically.
insights.locator: That was 2014. Now, in the last few years

insights.claim: Omand distinguishes ethical difficulty by domain: maritime settings, such as a tethered sea mine in a declared minefield, pose far less complex ethical challenges than an autonomous system operating in a crowded city.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Suggests autonomous-weapons approval will be sequenced by domain — maritime/naval autonomy is likely to be greenlit well before urban ground autonomy, a useful timeline marker for defence-AI investment.
insights.quote: The implications of a tethered sea mine in a declared minefield are not the same as the ethical understanding required of an autonomous system operating in a crowded city.
insights.locator: It is also the case that some environments of conflict

themes.theme: Formalising moral reasoning into auditable weights

insights.claim: Omand's proposed method does not invent new ethics but formalises existing laws-of-war concepts — necessity, proportionality, civilian protection, avoidance of collateral damage — into explicit, weighted trade-offs an AI can optimise, with the weights set and logged by a human commander.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Describes the actual technical mechanism proposed for legal compliance — a weighted-utility architecture policymakers and defence-AI vendors are likely to converge on as the compliance standard.
insights.quote: advanced AI can make those weights explicit, logged and auditable
insights.locator: A trained AI system ought to be able to optimise

insights.claim: Logged, parameterised AI weight functions make moral accountability more traceable than human combat decisions, because responsibility falls on 'the commander who set the weights' rather than being reconstructed after the fact from an individual's split-second judgment.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Reframes liability exposure in autonomous warfare toward whoever configures the system, with implications for command liability and defence-contractor indemnification structures.
insights.quote: Who is accountable when the machine gets it wrong? The commander who set the weights.
insights.locator: Who is accountable when the machine gets it wrong?

themes.theme: The strategic pressure: Russia, China, no realistic treaty

insights.claim: Both Russia and China have already fielded autonomous weapons systems without a human in the loop for lethal-force decisions, and are investing heavily in drone-swarm 'hive mind' technology where targeting is negotiated collectively among the drones themselves.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: A direct claim that peer adversaries have already crossed the full-autonomy threshold — the strongest argument in the piece for Western defence-AI investment and a key marker for the state of the autonomous-weapons arms race.
insights.quote: Both Russia and China have already fielded autonomous weapons systems that do not have a human in the loop

insights.claim: Omand sees no realistic prospect of an international treaty banning autonomous weapons systems, given that Russia and China are already fielding and developing them.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Forecloses a 'ban via treaty' outcome as investable policy — implies continued and likely accelerating national investment in lethal AI rather than multilateral restraint.
insights.quote: I do not see a realistic possibility of an international treaty to ban autonomous weapons systems.
insights.locator: I do not think so, given that Russia and China

insights.claim: Omand cites Israel's Iron Dome and the war in Ukraine as existing real-world cases where the compressed time available for defence already approaches machine-speed decision-making.
insights.speaker: David Omand
insights.so_what: Grounds the autonomy debate in already-operational systems rather than hypotheticals — Iron Dome-style rapid-response air defence is a template likely to be replicated and exported.
insights.locator: The same will be true of advanced air defence
